revision.comment: [[島根県]][[出雲市]]にある[[スセリビメ]]を祀る神社
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 那売佐神社 |画像 = [[ファイル:Namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|那売佐神社本殿]]
本殿 |所在地 = 島根県出雲市東神西町720 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|18|51|N|132|42|0|E|}} |祭神 = 葦原醜男命
須勢理姫命 |社格 = 式内社
郷社 |創建 = |本殿 = 大鳥造 |別名 = 高倉明神 |例祭 = 4月23日 |神事 = }} '''那売佐神社'''（なめさじんじゃ、那賣佐神社）は[[島根県]][[出雲市]]東神西町にある[[神社]]である。[[式内社]]で旧社格は郷社。[[神西湖]]の南東にある高倉山のふもとに鎮座する。 == 祭神 == * 葦原醜男命（[[大国主]]の別名） * [[スセリビメ|須勢理姫命]] == 歴史 == 『[[出雲国風土記]]』[[神門郡]]条に在神祇官社の「奈賣佐社」と「那賣佐社」の2社が記載されている。『[[延喜式神名帳]]』出雲国神門郡には「那賣佐神社」と「同社坐和加須西利比売神社」の2社の記載があり、この時点ですでに2つの「ナメサ社」が合祀されていたと考えられる。 [[天和 (日本)|天和]]3年（1683年）に成立した『出雲風土記鈔』には俗に「岩坪大明神」と呼ばれていると記されている。[[享保]]2年（1717年）に成立した『雲陽誌』には「高倉明神」という社名で俗に「岩坪明神」と呼ぶと記載されている。[[永禄]]5年（1562年）の当社の棟札から[[室町時代]]後期にはすでに旧社地の岩坪（後述）から現社地に移転していたものとみられる関(2006)954頁。『雲陽誌』にはまた「波加佐社奈売佐社あり」と記載されていることから、『出雲国風土記』不在神祇官社の「波加佐社」の一つを合祀したとも考えられる関(2006)953頁。 明治5年に[[近代社格制度]]における郷社に認定された。その後の一時期に神西八幡宮を合祀していた[http://www.izumo.ne.jp/~jinzai-pc/son/html/03-05.html]。 == 境内 == * 拝殿 * 本殿 * 伊邪那岐命社 境内の裏から登山道を上がると山頂の[[神西城]]祉まで行ける。 == 岩坪 == [[ファイル:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|岩坪]] 『出雲国風土記』神門郡滑狭郷（なめさのさと）条に、所造天下大神（＝大国主）が和加須世理比売命（＝スセリビメ）のところへ[[妻問婚|妻問い]]に行ったときに、社の前に磐石があり、表面が滑らかだったことから大神が「滑磐石（なめしいわ）なるかも」と言い、それが郷名の由来となったという説話が記載されている。この説話に登場する「滑磐石」に比定されるのが現社地の近くを流れる九景川（[[十間川]]水系）の渓谷にある[[甌穴]]「岩坪」である。この付近に旧社地があると考えられ、近くには岩坪明神の[[祠]]が置かれている。岩坪は祭神の須勢理姫命が生誕時に産湯を使ったという地元の伝承がある[http://www.izumo.ne.jp/~jinzai-pc/map/html/no06.html]。 == 脚注 == == 参考文献 == * 関和彦『『出雲国風土記』註論』 2006年 [[明石書店]] ISBN 4-7503-2376-4 * 濱村台次郎『[http://www.izumo.ne.jp/~jinzai-pc/son/son.html 新訂 神西村史]』 1988年 神西公民館（原著は1934年） {{DEFAULTSORT:なめさしんしや}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:島根県の神社]] [[Category:式内小社]] [[Category:郷社]] [[Category:出雲市]]

revision.comment: 境外社と石くま之曽宮説について加筆
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 那売佐神社 |画像 = [[ファイル:Namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|那売佐神社本殿]]
本殿 |所在地 = 島根県出雲市東神西町720 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|18|51|N|132|42|0|E|}} |祭神 = 葦原醜男命
須勢理姫命 |社格 = 式内社
郷社 |創建 = |本殿 = 大鳥造 |別名 = 高倉明神 |例祭 = 4月23日 |神事 = }} '''那売佐神社'''（なめさじんじゃ、那賣佐神社）は[[島根県]][[出雲市]]東神西町にある[[神社]]である。[[式内社]]で旧社格は郷社。[[神西湖]]の南東にある高倉山のふもとに鎮座する。 == 祭神 == * 葦原醜男命（[[大国主]]の別名） * [[スセリビメ|須勢理姫命]] == 歴史 == 『[[出雲国風土記]]』[[神門郡]]条に在神祇官社の「奈賣佐社」と「那賣佐社」の2社が記載されている。『[[延喜式神名帳]]』出雲国神門郡には「那賣佐神社九条家本、武田家本、吉田本ではいずれも「那賣"伎"神社」と表記されており、これらは誤写と考えられている（ 『式内社調査報告 (21)』608頁 ）。」と「同社坐和加須西利比売神社」の2社の記載があり、この時点ですでに2つの「ナメサ社」が合祀されていたと考えられる。 [[天和 (日本)|天和]]3年（[[1683年]]）に成立した『出雲風土記鈔』には俗に「岩坪大明神」と呼ばれていると記されている。[[享保]]2年（[[1717年]]）に成立した『雲陽誌』には「高倉明神」という社名で俗に「岩坪明神」と呼ぶと記載されている。[[永禄]]5年（[[1562年]]）の当社の棟札から[[室町時代]]後期にはすでに旧社地の岩坪（後述）から現社地に移転していたものとみられる関(2006)954頁。『雲陽誌』にはまた「波加佐社奈売佐社あり」と記載されていることから、『出雲国風土記』不在神祇官社の「波加佐社」の一つを合祀したとも考えられる関(2006)953頁。 [[明治]]5年（[[1872年]]）に[[近代社格制度]]における郷社に認定された。 == 境内 == * 拝殿（入母屋造平入り、瓦葺） * 本殿（大社造変態、栃葺） * 伊邪那岐命社 境内の脇から登山道を上がると山頂の[[神西城]]祉まで行ける。 == 境外社 == ;神西八幡宮 :東神西町中組にある。[[貞応]]2年（[[1223年]]）に小野高通が[[地頭]]として神西村に赴任してきた翌年に氏神である[[鶴岡八幡宮]]の分霊を勧請してきたものとされる。江戸時代までは最大2[[町 (単位)|町]]7[[反]]あまりの社領を持ち、12か村の氏神となるほどの神社であったが、明治維新のときに無格社に列格され、明治43年（[[1910年]]）に境内社であった小野神社、稲荷神社、金刀比羅神社とともに当社に合祀された 『式内社調査報告(21)』609頁。 ;貴船神社 :東神西町高岩と井内の2箇所にある。いずれも明治43年に合祀されたものである。 == 岩坪 == [[ファイル:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|岩坪]] 『出雲国風土記』神門郡滑狭郷（なめさのさと）条に、所造天下大神（＝大国主）が和加須世理比売命（＝スセリビメ）のところへ[[妻問婚|妻問い]]に行ったときに、社の前に磐石があり、表面が滑らかだったことから大神が「滑磐石（なめしいわ）なるかも」と言い、それが郷名の由来となったという説話が記載されている。この説話に登場する「滑磐石」に比定されるのが現社地の近くを流れる九景川（[[十間川]]水系）の渓谷にある[[甌穴]]「岩坪」である。この付近に旧社地があると考えられ、近くには岩坪明神の[[祠]]が置かれている。岩坪は祭神の須勢理姫命が生誕時に産湯を使ったという地元の伝承がある[http://www.izumo.ne.jp/~jinzai-pc/map/html/no06.html]。 == 異説 == [[本居宣長]]は『[[古事記伝]]』において『[[古事記]]』の[[誉津別命|本牟智和気命]]の説話に登場する「石くま之曽宮「いわくまのそのみや」と訓ずる。「くま」は「石偏に冋」。」を[[内山真龍]]が『出雲国風土記』の滑狭郷の説話に基づいて当社に比定している説を援用し、[[出雲大社|杵築大社]]よりもむしろ当社のことではないかとしている 『式内社調査報告(21)』610頁。 == 脚注 == == 参考文献 == * 式内社研究会編『[[式内社調査報告]] 第二十一巻 出雲(B)・石見・隠岐』（当該項目の執筆者:藪信男） 1983年 [[皇學館大学|皇學館大學]]出版部 * 関和彦『『出雲国風土記』註論』 2006年 [[明石書店]] ISBN 4-7503-2376-4 * 濱村台次郎『[http://www.izumo.ne.jp/~jinzai-pc/son/son.html 新訂 神西村史]』 1988年 神西公民館（原著は1934年） {{DEFAULTSORT:なめさしんしや}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:島根県の神社]] [[Category:式内小社]] [[Category:郷社]] [[Category:出雲市]]

須勢理姫命 |社格 = 式内社

== 脚注 == == 参考文献 == * 式内社研究会編『[[式内社調査報告]] 第二十一巻 出雲(B)・石見・隠岐』（当該項目の執筆者:藪信男） 1983年 [[皇學館大学|皇學館大學]]出版部 * 関和彦『『出雲国風土記』註論』 2006年 [[明石書店]] ISBN 4-7503-2376-4 * [[Category:出雲市]]

revision.comment: /* 参考文献 */ 式内社のカテゴリ変更。

(単位)|町]]7[[反]]あまりの社領を持ち、12か村の氏神となるほどの神社であったが、明治維新のときに無格社に列格され、明治43年（[[1910年]]）に境内社であった小野神社、稲荷神社、金刀比羅神社とともに当社に合祀された 『式内社調査報告(21)』609頁。 ;貴船神社 :東神西町高岩と井内の2箇所にある。いずれも明治43年に合祀されたものである。 == 岩坪 == [[ファイル:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|岩坪]] 『出雲国風土記』神門郡滑狭郷（なめさのさと）条に、所造天下大神（＝大国主）が和加須世理比売命（＝スセリビメ）のところへ[[妻問婚|妻問い]]に行ったときに、社の前に磐石があり、表面が滑らかだったことから大神が「滑磐石（なめしいわ）なるかも」と言い、それが郷名の由来となったという説話が記載されている。この説話に登場する「滑磐石」に比定されるのが現社地の近くを流れる九景川（[[十間川]]水系）の渓谷にある[[甌穴]]「岩坪」である。この付近に旧社地があると考えられ、近くには岩坪明神の[[祠]]が置かれている。岩坪は祭神の須勢理姫命が生誕時に産湯を使ったという地元の伝承がある[http://www.izumo.ne.jp/~jinzai-pc/map/html/no06.html]。 == 異説 == [[本居宣長]]は『[[古事記伝]]』において『[[古事記]]』の[[誉津別命|本牟智和気命]]の説話に登場する「石くま之曽宮「いわくまのそのみや」と訓ずる。「くま」は「石偏に冋」。」を[[内山真龍]]が『出雲国風土記』の滑狭郷の説話に基づいて当社に比定している説を援用し、[[出雲大社|杵築大社]]よりもむしろ当社のことではないかとしている 『式内社調査報告(21)』610頁。 == 脚注 == == 参考文献 == * 式内社研究会編『[[式内社調査報告]] 第二十一巻 出雲(B)・石見・隠岐』（当該項目の執筆者:藪信男） 1983年 [[皇學館大学|皇學館大學]]出版部 * 関和彦『『出雲国風土記』註論』 2006年 [[明石書店]] ISBN 4-7503-2376-4 * 濱村台次郎『[http://www.izumo.ne.jp/~jinzai-pc/son/son.html 新訂 神西村史]』 1988年 神西公民館（原著は1934年） {{DEFAULTSORT:なめさしんしや}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:島根県の神社]] [[Category:出雲国の式内社]] [[Category:島根県の旧郷社]] [[Category:出雲市]]

{{神社 |名称 = 那売佐神社 |画像 = [[ファイル:Namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|那売佐神社本殿]]
本殿 |所在地 = 島根県出雲市東神西町720 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|18|51|N|132|42|0|E|}} |祭神 = 葦原醜男命
須勢理姫命 |社格 = 式内社
郷社 |創建 = |本殿 = 大鳥造 |別名 = 高倉明神 |例祭 = 4月23日 |神事 = }} '''那売佐神社'''（なめさじんじゃ、那賣佐神社）は[[島根県]][[出雲市]]東神西町にある[[神社]]である。[[式内社]]で旧社格は[[氏子調#行政単位と郷社|郷社]]。[[神西湖]]の南東にある高倉山のふもとに鎮座する。 == 祭神 == * 葦原醜男命（[[大国主]]の別名） * [[スセリビメ|須勢理姫命]] == 歴史 == 『[[出雲国風土記]]』[[神門郡]]条に在神祇官社の「奈賣佐社」と「那賣佐社」の2社が記載されている。『[[延喜式神名帳]]』出雲国神門郡には「那賣佐神社九条家本、武田家本、吉田本ではいずれも「那賣"伎"神社」と表記されており、これらは誤写と考えられている（ 『式内社調査報告 (21)』608頁 ）。」と「同社坐和加須西利比売神社」の2社の記載があり、この時点ですでに2つの「ナメサ社」が合祀されていたと考えられる。 [[天和 (日本)|天和]]3年（[[1683年]]）に成立した『出雲風土記鈔』には俗に「岩坪大明神」と呼ばれていると記されている。[[享保]]2年（[[1717年]]）に成立した『雲陽誌』には「高倉明神」という社名で俗に「岩坪明神」と呼ぶと記載されている。[[永禄]]5年（[[1562年]]）の当社の棟札から[[室町時代]]後期にはすでに旧社地の岩坪（後述）から現社地に移転していたものとみられる関(2006)954頁。『雲陽誌』にはまた「波加佐社奈売佐社あり」と記載されていることから、『出雲国風土記』不在神祇官社の「波加佐社」の一つを合祀したとも考えられる関(2006)953頁。 [[明治]]5年（[[1872年]]）に[[近代社格制度]]における郷社に認定された。 == 境内 == * 拝殿（入母屋造平入り、瓦葺） * 本殿（大社造変態、栃葺） * 伊邪那岐命社 境内の脇から登山道を上がると山頂の[[神西城]]祉まで行ける。 == 境外社 == ;神西八幡宮 :東神西町中組にある。[[貞応]]2年（[[1223年]]）に小野高通が[[地頭]]として神西村に赴任してきた翌年に氏神である[[鶴岡八幡宮]]の分霊を勧請してきたものとされる。江戸時代までは最大2[[町 (単位)|町]]7[[反]]あまりの社領を持ち、12か村の氏神となるほどの神社であったが、明治維新のときに無格社に列格され、明治43年（[[1910年]]）に境内社であった小野神社、稲荷神社、金刀比羅神社とともに当社に合祀された 『式内社調査報告(21)』609頁。 ;貴船神社 :東神西町高岩と井内の2箇所にある。いずれも明治43年に合祀されたものである。 == 岩坪 == [[ファイル:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|岩坪]] 『出雲国風土記』神門郡滑狭郷（なめさのさと）条に、所造天下大神（＝大国主）が和加須世理比売命（＝スセリビメ）のところへ[[妻問婚|妻問い]]に行ったときに、社の前に磐石があり、表面が滑らかだったことから大神が「滑磐石（なめしいわ）なるかも」と言い、それが郷名の由来となったという説話が記載されている。この説話に登場する「滑磐石」に比定されるのが現社地の近くを流れる九景川（[[十間川]]水系）の渓谷にある[[甌穴]]「岩坪」である。この付近に旧社地があると考えられ、近くには岩坪明神の[[祠]]が置かれている。岩坪は祭神の須勢理姫命が生誕時に産湯を使ったという地元の伝承がある[http://www.izumo.ne.jp/~jinzai-pc/map/html/no06.html]。 == 異説 == [[本居宣長]]は『[[古事記伝]]』において『[[古事記]]』の[[誉津別命|本牟智和気命]]の説話に登場する「石くま之曽宮「いわくまのそのみや」と訓ずる。「くま」は「石偏に冋」。」を[[内山真龍]]が『出雲国風土記』の滑狭郷の説話に基づいて当社に比定している説を援用し、[[出雲大社|杵築大社]]よりもむしろ当社のことではないかとしている 『式内社調査報告(21)』610頁。 == 脚注 == == 参考文献 == * 式内社研究会編『[[式内社調査報告]] 第二十一巻 出雲(B)・石見・隠岐』（当該項目の執筆者:藪信男） 1983年 [[皇學館大学|皇學館大學]]出版部 * 関和彦『『出雲国風土記』註論』 2006年 [[明石書店]] ISBN 4-7503-2376-4 * 濱村台次郎『[http://www.izumo.ne.jp/~jinzai-pc/son/son.html 新訂 神西村史]』 1988年 神西公民館（原著は1934年） {{DEFAULTSORT:なめさしんしや}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:島根県の神社]] [[Category:出雲国の式内社]] [[Category:島根県の旧郷社]] [[Category:出雲市]]

revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 那売佐神社 |画像 = [[ファイル:Namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|那売佐神社本殿]]
本殿 |所在地 = 島根県出雲市東神西町720 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|18|51|N|132|42|0|E|}} |祭神 = 葦原醜男命
須勢理姫命 |社格 = 式内社
郷社 |創建 = |本殿 = 大鳥造 |別名 = 高倉明神 |例祭 = 4月23日 |神事 = }} '''那売佐神社'''（なめさじんじゃ、那賣佐神社）は[[島根県]][[出雲市]]東神西町にある[[神社]]である。[[式内社]]で旧社格は[[郷社]]。[[神西湖]]の南東にある高倉山のふもとに鎮座する。 == 祭神 == * 葦原醜男命（[[大国主]]の別名） * [[スセリビメ|須勢理姫命]] == 歴史 == 『[[出雲国風土記]]』[[神門郡]]条に在神祇官社の「奈賣佐社」と「那賣佐社」の2社が記載されている。『[[延喜式神名帳]]』出雲国神門郡には「那賣佐神社九条家本、武田家本、吉田本ではいずれも「那賣"伎"神社」と表記されており、これらは誤写と考えられている（ 『式内社調査報告 (21)』608頁 ）。」と「同社坐和加須西利比売神社」の2社の記載があり、この時点ですでに2つの「ナメサ社」が合祀されていたと考えられる。 [[天和 (日本)|天和]]3年（[[1683年]]）に成立した『出雲風土記鈔』には俗に「岩坪大明神」と呼ばれていると記されている。[[享保]]2年（[[1717年]]）に成立した『雲陽誌』には「高倉明神」という社名で俗に「岩坪明神」と呼ぶと記載されている。[[永禄]]5年（[[1562年]]）の当社の棟札から[[室町時代]]後期にはすでに旧社地の岩坪（後述）から現社地に移転していたものとみられる関(2006)954頁。『雲陽誌』にはまた「波加佐社奈売佐社あり」と記載されていることから、『出雲国風土記』不在神祇官社の「波加佐社」の一つを合祀したとも考えられる関(2006)953頁。 [[明治]]5年（[[1872年]]）に[[近代社格制度]]における郷社に認定された。 == 境内 == * 拝殿（入母屋造平入り、瓦葺） * 本殿（大社造変態、栃葺） * 伊邪那岐命社 境内の脇から登山道を上がると山頂の[[神西城]]祉まで行ける。 == 境外社 == ;神西八幡宮 :東神西町中組にある。[[貞応]]2年（[[1223年]]）に小野高通が[[地頭]]として神西村に赴任してきた翌年に氏神である[[鶴岡八幡宮]]の分霊を勧請してきたものとされる。江戸時代までは最大2[[町 (単位)|町]]7[[反]]あまりの社領を持ち、12か村の氏神となるほどの神社であったが、明治維新のときに無格社に列格され、明治43年（[[1910年]]）に境内社であった小野神社、稲荷神社、金刀比羅神社とともに当社に合祀された 『式内社調査報告(21)』609頁。 ;貴船神社 :東神西町高岩と井内の2箇所にある。いずれも明治43年に合祀されたものである。 == 岩坪 == [[ファイル:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|岩坪]] 『出雲国風土記』神門郡滑狭郷（なめさのさと）条に、所造天下大神（＝大国主）が和加須世理比売命（＝スセリビメ）のところへ[[妻問婚|妻問い]]に行ったときに、社の前に磐石があり、表面が滑らかだったことから大神が「滑磐石（なめしいわ）なるかも」と言い、それが郷名の由来となったという説話が記載されている。この説話に登場する「滑磐石」に比定されるのが現社地の近くを流れる九景川（[[十間川]]水系）の渓谷にある[[甌穴]]「岩坪」である。この付近に旧社地があると考えられ、近くには岩坪明神の[[祠]]が置かれている。岩坪は祭神の須勢理姫命が生誕時に産湯を使ったという地元の伝承がある[http://www.izumo.ne.jp/~jinzai-pc/map/html/no06.html]。 == 異説 == [[本居宣長]]は『[[古事記伝]]』において『[[古事記]]』の[[誉津別命|本牟智和気命]]の説話に登場する「石くま之曽宮「いわくまのそのみや」と訓ずる。「くま」は「石偏に冋」。」を[[内山真龍]]が『出雲国風土記』の滑狭郷の説話に基づいて当社に比定している説を援用し、[[出雲大社|杵築大社]]よりもむしろ当社のことではないかとしている 『式内社調査報告(21)』610頁。 == 脚注 == == 参考文献 == * 式内社研究会編『[[式内社調査報告]] 第二十一巻 出雲(B)・石見・隠岐』（当該項目の執筆者:藪信男） 1983年 [[皇學館大学|皇學館大學]]出版部 * 関和彦『『出雲国風土記』註論』 2006年 [[明石書店]] ISBN 4-7503-2376-4 * 濱村台次郎『[http://www.izumo.ne.jp/~jinzai-pc/son/son.html 新訂 神西村史]』 1988年 神西公民館（原著は1934年） {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:なめさしんしや}} [[Category:島根県の神社]] [[Category:出雲国の式内社]] [[Category:島根県の旧郷社]] [[Category:出雲市の歴史]]

revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = Namesa Shrine | Image = [[File:Namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of Namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | Location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | Coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | Deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''Namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "Namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two Namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with Namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with Namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "Nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be Namesa Shrine instead of the nearby {{ill|Izumo Grand Shrine|ja|杵築大社}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Shrines in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Izumo Province]] [[Category:Former Gōsha]] [[Category:Buildings in Izumo City]] [[Category:History of Izumo City]] [[ja:那売佐神社]]

{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''Namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "Namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two Namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with Namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with Namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "Nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be Namesa Shrine instead of the nearby {{ill|Izumo Grand Shrine|ja|杵築大社}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Shrines in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Shikinaisha of Izumo Province]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Former Gōsha]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Buildings in Izumo City]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:History of Izumo City]] [[ja:那売佐神社]]

In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with Namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with Namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "Nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be Namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Shrines in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Shikinaisha of Izumo Province]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Former Gōsha]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Buildings in Izumo City]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:History of Izumo City]] [[ja:那売佐神社]]

Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be Namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Shrine | Name = Namesa Shrine | Image = [[File:Namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of Namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | Location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | Coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | Deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''Namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "Namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two Namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with Namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with Namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "Nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be Namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Pages with redlinks to revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = Namesa Shrine | image = [[File:Namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of Namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | Coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''Namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "Namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two Namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with Namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with Namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "Nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be Namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Pages with redlinks to This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be Namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Shrine | Name = Namesa Shrine | image = [[File:Namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of Namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | Coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''Namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "Namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two Namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with Namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with Namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "Nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be Namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = Namesa Shrine | image = [[File:Namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of Namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | Coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''Namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "Namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two Namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with Namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with Namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "Nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be Namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:unsorted link {{Shrine | Name = Namesa Shrine | image = [[File:Namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of Namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | Coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''Namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "Namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two Namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with Namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with Namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "Nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be Namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages {{Shrine | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:pages needing revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Nausa revision.comment: Adding [[Category:q-{{subst:NAMESPACE}}-1]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Nausa Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted revision.comment: Removing this excess
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Nausa Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Nausa Shrine-qq]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Q--1]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq deprecated start]][[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq deprecated This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References ==
