revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})

Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|出雲国風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Nausa Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = namesa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:namesajinjahonden.JPG|180px|Main hall of namesa Shrine]]
Main hall | location = {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県出雲市東神西町}} 720 | coordinates = {{ill|35°18′51″N 132°42′0″E|ja}} | deity = {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}})
{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Nausa Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shrines in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Izumo Province]] [[Category:Former Gōsha]] [[Category:Buildings in Izumo City]] [[Category:History of Izumo City]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:Nausa Shrine]] [[ja:那売佐神社]] [[en:Nausa Shrine]]

Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治|12=draft}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|12=draft}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城|12=draft}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城|12=draft}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村|12=draft}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応|12=draft}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治|12=draft}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記|12=draft}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命|12=draft}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川|12=draft}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|12=draft}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Nausa Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shrines in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Izumo Province]] [[Category:Former Gōsha]] [[Category:Buildings in Izumo City]] [[Category:History of Izumo City]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:Nausa Shrine]] [[ja:那売佐神社]] [[en:Nausa Shrine]]

It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|12=draft}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖|12=draft}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命|12=draft}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命|12=draft}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記|12=draft}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡|12=draft}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和|12=draft}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔|12=draft}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保|12=draft}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治|12=draft}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|12=draft}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城|12=draft}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城|12=draft}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村|12=draft}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応|12=draft}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治|12=draft}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記|12=draft}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命|12=draft}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川|12=draft}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|12=draft}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Nausa Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shrines in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Izumo Province]] [[Category:Former Gōsha]] [[Category:Buildings in Izumo City]] [[Category:History of Izumo City]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:Nausa Shrine]] [[ja:那売佐神社]] [[en:Nausa Shrine]]

This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川|12=draft}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|12=draft}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Nausa Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shrines in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Izumo Province]] [[Category:Go-sha]] [[Category:Buildings in Izumo City]] [[Category:History of Izumo City]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] }} [[sn:Nausa Shrine]] [[ja:那売佐神社]] [[en:Nausa Shrine]]

{{ill|Suserihime-no-Mikoto|ja|須勢理姫命|12=draft}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft}}
{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|12=draft}} | Main Hall = {{ill|Ōtori-zukuri|ja|大鳥造|12=draft}} | Other names = Takakura Myōjin | Main Festival = April 23 }} '''namesa Shrine''' (那売佐神社, なめさじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Higashi Jinsei Town, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture|ja|出雲市東神西町|12=draft}}, Japan. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|12=draft}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖|12=draft}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命|12=draft}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命|12=draft}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記|12=draft}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡|12=draft}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}} period, the shrine had already merged two namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和|12=draft}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔|12=draft}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保|12=draft}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治|12=draft}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|12=draft}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城|12=draft}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城|12=draft}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村|12=draft}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応|12=draft}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治|12=draft}}, it was merged with namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記|12=draft}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命|12=draft}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川|12=draft}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|12=draft}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Nausa Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shrines in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Izumo Province]] [[Category:Go-sha]] [[Category:Buildings in Izumo City]] [[Category:History of Izumo City]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Nausa Shrine]] [[ja:那売佐神社]] [[en:Nausa Shrine]]

It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft}} with the former shrine rank of {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|12=draft}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of Takakura Mountain, southeast of {{ill|Lake Jinsei|ja|神西湖|12=draft}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Ashihara no Shikoo|ja|葦原醜男命|12=draft}} (an epithet of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命|12=draft}} == History == The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記|12=draft}} under the {{ill|Kamimiguni District|ja|神門郡|12=draft}} entry as "Nasa Shrine" (奈賣佐社) and "Namesa Shrine" (那賣佐社). By the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}} period, the shrine had already merged two Namesa Shrines into one. In {{ill|Tenna Era|ja|天和|12=draft}} 3 (1683), the {{ill|Izumo Fudokishō|ja|出雲風土記鈔|12=draft}} refers to the shrine as "Iwatsubo Myōjin," and by {{ill|Kyōhō Era|ja|享保|12=draft}} 2 (1717), it was called "Takakura Myōjin." Records suggest the shrine was relocated from its original site, Iwatsubo, to the current location during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治|12=draft}}, it was designated a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|12=draft}} under the modern shrine ranking system. == Grounds == * Haiden (worship hall): Irimoya-zukuri, tiled roof * Honden (main hall): Modified Taisha-zukuri, bark thatched roof * Izanagi Shrine A trail starting from the shrine grounds leads to the summit of {{ill|Mount Jinsei|ja|神西城|12=draft}}, the site of the historical {{ill|Jinsei Castle|ja|神西城|12=draft}}. == Sub-Shrines == ;Jinsei Hachiman Shrine : Located in Higashi Jinsei Town Nakagumi, it was originally enshrined in {{ill|Jinsei Village|ja|神西村|12=draft}} in {{ill|Jinmei Era|ja|貞応|12=draft}} 2 (1223) by the local steward Ono Takamichi. In the {{ill|Meiji Era|ja|明治|12=draft}}, it was merged with Namesa Shrine along with its subsidiary shrines. ;Kifune Shrine : Located in two locations in Higashi Jinsei Town: Takaiwa and Inochi. Both were merged with Namesa Shrine in 1910. == Iwatsubo == [[File:Izumoiwatsubo.JPG|thumb|Iwatsubo]] The {{ill|Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記|12=draft}} states that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft}} visited {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理姫命|12=draft}} at this site and remarked on a smooth rock, naming it "nameru Iwa" (滑磐石). This rock, identified as the pothole "Iwatsubo" in the nearby {{ill|Kukegawa River|ja|九景川|12=draft}}, is considered the origin of the local area's name. == Alternative Theories == {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|12=draft}} proposed that the shrine mentioned in {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} as "Iwakuma no Sono Miya" (石くま之曽宮) might be Namesa Shrine instead of the nearby [[Izumo-taisha]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Shikinaisha Research Society, "Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 21: Izumo (B), Iwami, Oki," 皇學館大学出版部, 1983. * Sekikazu, "Izumo Fudoki Annotated Commentary," 明石書店, 2006, ISBN 4-7503-2376-4. * Hamamura Daijirō, "Revised History of Jinsei Village," Jinsei Public Hall, 1988 (original publication: 1934). {{shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Namesa Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shrines in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Izumo Province]] [[Category:Go-sha]] [[Category:Buildings in Izumo City]] [[Category:History of Izumo City]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Namesa Shrine]] [[ja:那売佐神社]] [[en:Namesa Shrine]]
